data.title: Avarda Bank (OM:AVARDA) Q1 EPS Strength Tests Bearish Credit Risk Narrative
data.description:
Avarda Bank (OM:AVARDA) opened Q1 2026 with total revenue of SEK 536.8 million and basic EPS of SEK 3.17, while on a trailing twelve month basis revenue stood at SEK 2.0 billion and EPS at SEK 10.94, as the bank continued to convert top line into earnings. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from SEK 607.8 million in Q4 2024 to SEK 436.9 million in Q1 2025, then to SEK 536.8 million in Q1 2026, with basic EPS shifting from SEK 3.68 to SEK 2.15 and now SEK 3.17 over the same points. With a trailing net margin of 35.2% and earnings growth of 11.5% over the past year, investors are likely to focus on how sustainably Avarda is defending profitability.

See our full analysis for Avarda Bank.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up with the main bullish and bearish narratives that have built around Avarda over the past year.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Loan Book Growth to SEK 25.2b

  • Total loans reached SEK 25,231.6 million by Q4 2025, up from SEK 21,063.7 million in Q4 2024, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of SEK 2.0b and net income of SEK 706.9 million by Q1 2026.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is the combination of expanding lending volumes and earnings, with five year earnings growth averaging 23.8% a year. Investors still have to weigh that against the modest step down in net margin from 37.7% to 35.2%.
    • Supporters of a bullish case often point to that 23.8% multi year earnings growth and the Q1 2026 basic EPS of SEK 3.17 as evidence that Avarda has been able to turn a growing loan book into higher profits.
    • At the same time, the drop in net margin to 35.2% keeps the bullish side cautious because it shows profitability is strong but not moving in a straight line, which matters when growth is tied to consumer credit.

Non Performing Loans Reach 6.5%

  • Non performing loans rose from SEK 282.6 million in Q4 2024 to SEK 1,637.4 million in Q4 2025, which corresponds to a 6.5% bad loan ratio with allowance coverage at about 95%.
  • Bears focus on this credit profile, arguing that a high share of problem loans and less than full coverage can pressure future profitability, and the data here gives them several points to highlight.
    • Critics highlight that non performing loans have increased through 2025 while the allowance covers about 95% of those balances, so there is less of a cushion than if coverage sat comfortably above 100%.
    • What complicates the bearish story is that, even alongside those credit figures, trailing twelve month earnings still grew 11.5% and net profit margin remains a solid 35.2%, so the impact of those problem loans has not erased profitability in the reported period.
Stay on top of how credit trends feed into bull and bear debates around future profitability with the 🐻 Avarda Bank Bear Case.

P/E Premium vs DCF Fair Value

  • At a share price of SEK 181.20, Avarda trades on a P/E of 16.6x, above the European banks industry at 11.5x and peers at 11.7x, and also above a DCF fair value of SEK 145.56.
  • Supporters of a bullish angle often argue that strong historic and forecast growth can justify paying more. Yet the current premium to both peers and DCF fair value means the numbers are asking investors to have confidence that the growth and margins seen in the trailing twelve month data are worth more than SEK 145.56 per share.
    • On the growth side, trailing twelve month EPS of SEK 10.94 and revenue of SEK 2.0b, together with the 11.5% earnings growth rate, are the kind of figures bulls cite when explaining why the stock does not sit on a sector average multiple.
    • On the valuation side, the gap between SEK 181.20 and the DCF fair value level suggests that if earnings or margins soften, investors do not have a wide valuation buffer compared with peers trading closer to 11.5x to 11.7x P/E.
If you want a broader context on how investors weigh growth, valuation, and risk across companies, have a look at the Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives.

Next Steps

Don't just look at this quarter; the real story is in the long-term trend. We've done an in-depth analysis on Avarda Bank's growth and its valuation to see if today's price is a bargain. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio now so you don't miss the next big move.

With both tailwinds and pressure points in play, the real question is how you see the balance of risk and reward for Avarda right now. To test your view against the underlying numbers, take a closer look at the 3 key rewards and 2 important warning signs.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Sterling Infrastructure (STRL) After A 225% One Year Surge?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Sterling Infrastructure's current share price still offers value after a strong run, the key is to look closely at what the market might already be pricing in.
  • The stock closed at US$456.08, with returns of 7.7% over 7 days, 9.2% over 30 days, 42.9% year to date and 224.8% over 1 year. This naturally raises questions about how much optimism is now embedded in the price and whether risk perceptions have shifted.
  • Recent attention around Sterling Infrastructure has focused on its role within the broader capital goods space and how investors are reassessing companies tied to large scale infrastructure spending. This has put a spotlight on how the company might be positioned if funding, project pipelines or policy priorities change, all of which feed directly into how the stock is being valued.
  • Despite this strong performance, Sterling Infrastructure currently scores 0 out of 6 on Simply Wall St's valuation checks. Approach 1: Sterling Infrastructure Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A DCF model takes projected future cash flows and then discounts them back to today to estimate what the entire business might be worth in current dollars.

For Sterling Infrastructure, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow (FCF) is reported at about $355.7 million. Analyst and model projections used here point to FCF of $433.9 million in 2026 and $484 million by 2030, all in $. Estimates beyond the first few years are extrapolated using Simply Wall St's 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model.

When all these projected cash flows are discounted back and added together, the model arrives at an intrinsic value of about $261.48 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $456.08, this implies the stock is around 74.4% overvalued on this DCF view.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: Sterling Infrastructure Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to connect what you pay for each share with the earnings that support that price. It helps you see how many dollars of share price the market is willing to assign to each dollar of current earnings.

What counts as a "normal" or "fair" P/E generally reflects two things: how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings might be. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk often justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower multiple.

Sterling Infrastructure currently trades on a P/E of 48.23x. That sits above the Construction industry average P/E of 38.08x and the peer group average of 30.97x. data.title: SAP BlueSnap Integration Ties Cloud ERP To Stickier Payments Story
data.description:
• SAP (XTRA:SAP) has introduced a native direct to core payment integration with BlueSnap for SAP Cloud ERP and S/4HANA.
  • The new integration removes the need for third party payment connectors and is available as a certified solution for SAP enterprise customers.
  • The launch is aimed at simplifying global payment processing, reconciliation, and compliance for multinational operations.

SAP, trading at €146.54, is moving to tighten the link between its core ERP platforms and global payment flows at a time when many enterprises are looking to simplify cross border commerce. The stock shows a mixed performance profile, with a 31.3% return over 3 years and 36.3% over 5 years, set against a 27.4% decline year to date and a 36.4% decline over 1 year. For investors watching XTRA:SAP, this kind of product move is directly aligned with SAP’s core strength of supporting complex global operations.

For enterprise clients already standardised on SAP, a certified, direct connection to BlueSnap could help reduce reliance on custom payment builds and multiple intermediaries. Looking ahead, the key things to watch are customer adoption of the integration, any visible impact on payment efficiency metrics, and how SAP positions similar partnerships across other financial workflows.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for SAP by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on SAP.

We've flagged 0 risks for SAP. See which could impact your investment.

This BlueSnap integration sits squarely in SAP’s push to make its cloud ERP offerings more of a one stop operating hub for global enterprises. By plugging a payment provider directly into SAP Cloud ERP and S/4HANA, customers can run payment processing, reconciliation, fraud checks, and tokenized transactions without layering separate connectors on top. For companies with multi entity, multi currency structures, that kind of simplification can be attractive when comparing SAP against suites from Oracle or Microsoft, or against more modular setups that stitch together several vendors.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The SAP Narrative

  • The move supports the narrative that SAP wants deeper integration into customer workflows, with payments data flowing directly into core ERP and potentially reinforcing higher recurring revenue from its cloud platforms.
  • It also tests the narrative’s assumption that SAP can keep execution tight while restructuring for AI, because complex, real time payment flows leave less room for integration issues or downtime.
  • The partnership model with a payment specialist like BlueSnap, including PCI compliant processing and local acquiring coverage, is not deeply detailed in the narrative and may add nuance to how SAP leans on partners rather than building everything in house.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for SAP to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • Execution risk if large multinational customers face disruption during migration from legacy payment service providers or from existing third party connectors.
  • Competitive pressure if integrated offerings from Oracle, Microsoft, or payment players like Adyen and Stripe are perceived as more flexible or easier to implement.
  • Potential for tighter payment and ERP integration to make SAP stickier within large accounts, supporting longer contract terms and higher upsell potential.
  • The PCI compliant, tokenization ready setup with fraud protection and multi entity support could appeal to complex global customers that value a single account structure for payments.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on how often SAP or BlueSnap reference customer wins that explicitly use this direct to core integration, any commentary on improved authorization rates or lower cross border costs, and whether SAP repeats this pattern across other financial workflows. Also watch how often payments are highlighted during SAP’s earnings calls or events like Sapphire, especially in comparison with cloud ERP and AI messaging, because that will signal how central these payment partnerships are becoming in SAP’s wider product story.

data.description:

The XINYUE approval lands at a time when the share price has climbed to HK$39.4, with a 30 day share price return of 21.6% and a 1 year total shareholder return of 82.34%. This suggests recent momentum on top of already strong longer term gains.

Preferred P/E of 37.4x, Is it justified?

On a P/E of 37.4x at HK$39.4, Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group sits well above both its own fair value estimate and the peer group, which points to a rich valuation.

The P/E multiple compares the share price to earnings per share and is a quick way to see how much you are paying for each unit of profit. For a pharmaceutical company with meaningful earnings, a higher P/E often reflects investor expectations for future growth, product approvals or sustained profitability.

In Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group's case, several factors point to the market paying up. Earnings grew 27.1% over the past year, ahead of its 5 year average of 17.4% and ahead of the Hong Kong Pharmaceuticals industry, which saw a 1.2% decline. Profit margins are currently 37%, above last year's 35.7%, and earnings growth over the past five years has averaged 17.4% per year. The SWS DCF model also indicates the shares trade about 22.8% below an estimated future cash flow value of HK$51.04, suggesting cash flow based valuation is more supportive than the current P/E might imply. However, the estimated fair P/E of 29x sits well below the current 37.4x. This highlights how far sentiment has moved above what the regression based fair ratio suggests could be a more grounded level if expectations cool.

Compared with peers, the premium is even starker. Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group's 37.4x P/E is higher than the Hong Kong Pharmaceuticals industry average of 15.2x and above the peer average of 24.3x. That gap indicates the market is assigning the company a premium multiple that is more than double the sector level and clearly above what the SWS fair ratio model indicates.

data.title: How Halliburton’s Vaca Muerta Electric Frac Deal At Halliburton (HAL) Has Changed Its Investment Story
data.description:
• On 13 April 2026, Halliburton announced an exclusive, multi-year multibillion-dollar contract from YPF to deliver bundled unconventional completions services in Argentina’s Vaca Muerta shale, including the first international deployment of its ZEUS electric fracturing and OCTIV digital fracturing technologies.
  • This deal not only deepens Halliburton’s role in one of the world’s largest shale resources outside North America, it also showcases how its electrified and automated fracturing solutions are becoming integral to lower-emissions, higher-efficiency oilfield operations.
  • We’ll now examine how this exclusive Vaca Muerta contract, especially the first international roll-out of ZEUS electric fracturing, influences Halliburton’s investment narrative.

Find 60 companies with promising cash flow potential yet trading below their fair value.

Advertisement

Halliburton Investment Narrative Recap

To own Halliburton, you largely have to believe that global oil and gas spending, especially in international and unconventional plays, remains healthy enough for its technology and services to stay in demand. The YPF Vaca Muerta win looks like a meaningful near term catalyst for that thesis, particularly around international growth and digital and electric fracturing uptake, but it does not remove the bigger risk that long run decarbonization and regulatory pressures could weigh on oilfield activity and margins.

data.title: Baudroieinc (TSE:4413) Earnings Growth Steady At 36.6% Reinforces Bullish Narratives
data.description:
baudroieinc (TSE:4413) has just closed FY 2026 with fourth quarter revenue of ¥5.1b and basic EPS of ¥24.23, capping a year in which trailing 12 month revenue reached ¥17.4b and EPS came in at ¥79.07. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from ¥3.2b in FY 2025 Q4 to ¥5.1b in FY 2026 Q4, while basic EPS moved from ¥17.29 to ¥24.23 over the same period. This gives investors a clear line of sight on how the top and bottom lines have tracked through the year. 36.6% earnings growth sets the tone

  • Over the last 12 months, net income reached ¥2,457 million and basic EPS on this basis was ¥79.07, compared with ¥1,799 million and ¥56.68 a year earlier, which lines up with the 36.6% trailing earnings growth figure.
  • Supporters of a bullish view often point to this 36.6% earnings growth and the move in trailing revenue from ¥11,649 million to ¥17,423 million as evidence of a business that is scaling. However, the step up in quarterly net income from ¥543.18 million in FY 2025 Q4 to ¥754.96 million in FY 2026 Q4 also means investors need to watch how consistently that level can be maintained.
    • Back to back quarters in FY 2026 show net income at ¥437.43 million, ¥576.57 million, ¥687.24 million and then ¥754.96 million, which fits a growth story but also raises the bar for future comparisons.
    • With trailing EPS now at ¥79.07, the bullish angle that the business has more earnings power than a year ago is grounded in hard numbers, not just forecasts.

Fans of balanced analysis who want to see how these growth numbers fit into a wider company view can check what other investors are focusing on through the 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about baudroieinc.

Margins hold at 14.1% as scale builds

  • Trailing net margin sits at 14.1% on ¥17,423 million of revenue, compared with 15.4% on ¥11,649 million a year earlier, so profit is higher in absolute yen terms even though each yen of sales now brings in a slightly smaller share of profit.
  • What stands out against a bullish angle is that while revenue over the trailing year rose from ¥11,649 million to ¥17,423 million and net income climbed from ¥1,799 million to ¥2,457 million, the margin slip from 15.4% to 14.1% shows that part of the growth story is coming from volume rather than fatter margins.
    • Quarterly data for FY 2026, with revenue running between ¥3,361.91 million and ¥5,099.47 million and net income between ¥437.43 million and ¥754.96 million, points to higher profit but not to widening margins in the latest snapshot.
    • Bulls who focus on revenue and EPS growth get support from the higher trailing totals, while the margin move gives a concrete number to track for quality of that growth.

P/E premium and DCF gap create valuation tension

  • At a share price of ¥2,277, baudroieinc trades on a trailing P/E of 28.9x against a peer average of 24.4x and a Japan IT industry average of 14.5x, yet the DCF fair value is set at ¥2,776.70, which is about 22% above the current price.
  • Critics who take a more bearish line often highlight the 28.9x P/E premium over peers and the move in net margin from 15.4% to 14.1% as signs that investors are paying up for growth that still has to be earned, while supporters point to the roughly ¥500 gap between the ¥2,277 market price and the ¥2,776.70 DCF fair value as a potential cushion.
    • The combination of 36.6% trailing earnings growth and forecasts calling for around 25.21% earnings growth and 27% revenue growth per year helps explain why the share trades above peer and sector P/E levels.
    • At the same time, recent share price volatility above the wider Japan market means any disappointment on growth or margins could matter more for holders who paid that premium multiple.
